transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hi, Cliff?
Yeah, hey, how are you doing today?
Hi, good.
How are you?
Sorry for a delay.
No worries.
How are you doing?
How's your day going?
Doing pretty well.
Still that back-to-work-at-the-beginning-of-the-year-bluster kind of thing going on, but otherwise, good.
How about yourself?
Same, same.
It's also a lot going on in the beginning of the year.
It's just like catching up with everyone and on other projects, etc.
So, busy times.
Busy times.
I'm glad you are here.
I'm glad we are having this interview.
Sorry.
So thanks for your time.
To introduce myself, my name is Katja.
I am currently advising GrowthX on data, and Daniel asked me to interview for data roles.
So I'm helping out with that today.
I am a full-stack data scientist.
My career spans over, you know, the health tech and fintech and some AI.
Right now, my full-time position is in fintech.
So very also broad and did a bunch of stuff from, you know, analytics, data engineering to ML and AI.
So really a broad range.
Yeah, I'm happy to interview today.
So my plan was, I'd like to learn more about you if you can.
And share more about your recent work and would love to dive into one of your projects, one of your recent projects.
And then I have a growthx key study I would love to talk through with you today.
And then we'll leave a couple of some minutes for questions.
Does that sound good?
Okay.
Yeah, absolutely.
Just let me know how far back you want me to go and what level of detail.
And I'll give you whatever level of information you need to see.
Sounds good.
Sounds good.
Yeah, maybe you just start the high level first and then we'll go from there.
Okay.
A high level, I've got a, I'm a data scientist that's moved into almost more machine learning engineering.
And then in the last year or so, much more of a full stack development kind of role with AI engineering coming front and center extensively in the last probably probably.
Yeah.
18 months working with LLM.
I started academically back with a PhD in social experimental psychology and a doctoral minor in stats.
Knew pretty quickly I didn't want to go into the academic route.

What the focus is of that, and I always say this as a disclaimer, most people hear psychology, they immediately think what kind of medical treatment someone walks in and sits on a couch and you talk to them.
But in graduate school, it immediately splits.

And you're either doing science, looking at the influences of information and information processing in the human brain as a scientist, not that dissimilar from a chemist or something like that, or you're doing treatment.
I was in the science side of it.

So social psych is essentially the science of how the environment and influences coming from information and other people change behavior and influence behavior and how to figure out if those changes occurred and identify what those effects are.
Right.
So that goes pretty heavily into marketing and on the government.
So I moved from there pretty quickly into government applications.
It was kind of the height of the war on terror.
I got picked up at Homeland Security for doctoral fellowship.
I worked at Los Alamos National Laboratory for about three years, both part-time and full-time on-site.
A couple of summers of graduate research intern, then I went to active duty military service.
I started right after I finished my PhD, really interested in doing tough guy stuff.
So I went from two straight years of training, almost 30, got first.
Sorry to interrupt you.
I hear like some, it's a little bit muffled.
So I'm not sure if it's on my side or on your side.
So it's hard to hear you.
Yeah.
Okay.
Let me try these.
These are pretty much full proof.
So I'll just transition really quickly.
My camera is really following me.
I'm suggesting my chair.
you hear better now?
Yes, much better.
Perfect.
That's perfect.
Okay.
Okay.
So after I moved out of government research and operational research, I immediately went into active duty military service.
I moved in to do what I jokingly call tough guy stuff.
So I was going through all the combat schools, moving into airborne ranger school, stuff like that.
And got hurt pretty quickly because I was almost 30.
And they said, back to the nerd stuff.
So I ended up at Aero Medical Research Laboratory.
So after about two years of training, then I went into two years of medical board and physical recovery.
It's easy to get pretty big injuries in that stuff.
And then started working with medical research publications.
And I kind of picked up there.
One of my favorite things had been putting together systems.
And my coding wasn't very good when I was in graduate school.
At Los Alamos National Lab, I actually got put on coding teams first exposed to it.
And then when I went back...
I realized that the statistical analyses, research methodology designs, and building tools to support those and automate those were kind of my favorite thing and where I excelled above peers.

After I got out, I went to do operational research and build additional systems for marksmanship capture and situational awareness optimization and looking at organizational impact across everything from like airborne schools to marksmanship schools to different training programs for all of the tough guy schools that I had just attended.
And then around there, hit the 10 or 11 year mark in government service and decided I wanted to migrate into corporate.
And about that time, I started doing some consulting and then migrated to move into what they were calling at the time data science within a place called Cenobus.
That's a southeast mid cap, they call banks, about a medium sized bank, five state footprint, about 50 billion in assets and help stand up their regulatory team.
I saw that you'd worked at, I think it was Mercury, which is a startup banking environment.
And this is going to be way more shackled down because they're more mature, more traditional.

So after 2008 recession came in, a bunch of regulations, particularly organizations like this, to take TARP bailouts, where if they wanted to inform their loan loss reserve, ALM, things of that nature, it used to just be they'd kind of say, well, our experts think here's what our losses are going to be.

The Federal Reserve stepped in and said, you're going to have to do something called DFAST, which means you're going to have to do quantitative modeling to support what it is that you're saying you should be holding back in your loan loss reserve and other things like that.
And so I was kind of the first person on the ground to initially establish what we call the regulatory team.
And we brought that up to then two or three people.
And honestly, I got so bored of building ARIMAs over and over and over and building thousands of pages of documentation, I automated the job away.
So I wouldn't have to do it anymore.
I figured they'd either fire me or let me do something else.
And they let me do other things.
How will you automate that type of work?
So instead of building ARIMAs, you would just automate building?
Right.

What I was able to do, it was, it ended up being really extensive in retrospect, but it kind of came piecemeal at the time where I started with, okay, this, this, this takes me because when I first came in, there were maybe 20 models Deloitte left behind and only maybe 100 pages documentation.
And as I'm looking at them, you know, we've got model risk management in there, they all would have failed.
I'm like, okay, first, we're going to step up and they were forcing me to use SAS.
So the first year and a half was, how do I get this over to R and or Python?
And then as I did that, how do I make proc ARIMA in R and Python?
Then it was automating.
Basically, you start with a module where you've got it in a workstation, but it does everything, but what needs to come to the surface.
So you're picking out, say, with an ARIMA, your, do we, what are the diagnostics?
Do we interpret it?
How do we pre-whiten usually through differencing?
What's the AR terms, MA terms?
Those are like only four or five parameters, but all the code bases want you to throw down a lot of codes.
You turn these into modules and an R, it's not going to load up like Python, right?
It's more source of script.
And you have a series of functions, same concept.
Then you start embedding it.
We were using this kind of latex wrapper in R called Knitter or something like that.
forget exactly what it was called, but it essentially allowed you to feature R code and the results of running the R code within a latex environment.
So you make the document template, which I'm not a big latex fan.
It does great things visually.
I hate playing in it.
Thankfully, we have things like Cloud Code for that now.
and then we basically made it so, well, one analyst can clear one of these models and or refresh it, back test it, and send it off to modernist management in about an hour.
So now, like, you've got the throughput up to, you can do hundreds and hundreds a year, you know, and they've got maybe a 20 or 30 page documentation footprint.
This is pre-LLMs being as widespread as theirs, like 10 years ago.

Well, the next step then becomes, I can standardize and make this kind of, I call it Mad Libs, games we played as kids where you'd have the little fill in an adjective and noun and and then you make sentences.
like, okay, I can use this copy and paste concatenation.
to then kind of automate the documentation of if this, then it says this in the documentation.

And the next step then became I had to build a model management system that I just built on Apache and Node with JavaScript jQuery heavy where I can put the information out so the management can go through, authorize, lock, unlock the model, review the forecast, download the forecast, upload the data, manually view the entire thing.
And then the analyst is just uploading it back and forth.
The next step for full automation came and we jokingly called it the ariminator.
But I realized that there were fairly low drag ways to be able to float to the top and the bottom based through a series of diagnostics, out of time testing like MAPE.
And then finally, we ended up building synthetic data where I randomized, I don't know, 5,000 scenario forecasts where they all would have basic versus severe and you would just look at them.
And I put in a densely connected network and we just sat down for two or three days and said, this is good, this is bad, this is good, this is bad.
Trained a deeply connected network to it to then rank order closer to one is better.
You have this overall metric of how the model's performing.

So then we would have it go through and train up to the top six or eight models that pass the overall what is the minimal acceptable, rank order them, surface it back to the management so they can look at it.
Because that honestly, the determination always came, does it pass diagnostics?
Do I like what the forecast say?
So then have it sit there and just turn and push out a hundred models overnight.
And each one of those management can go through and look at them and have the summary page in a server and say, I like this one best.
I pick it, it goes forward.
And the impact of that ended up being instead of them having to staff up, because, you know, the longer you're doing it, the more models you got to do.
So to, you know, nine or 10 people, they were able to keep it at two.

And then let me go over to start doing more interesting marketing and kind of prototyping research, what we call the development team, where we would do everything from trying to identify who should we bring into private wealth to how do we forecast if we're outside of expected balances, is there a run on the bank, even just the simplistic targeted marketing and personalization.
And from there, I ended up taking, I got poached away for a year at AT&T and then poached back somewhere in the middle of my 10 years at Synovus.
Mainly poached back because I was supposed to be remote off-site and I was not.
So I was up in Atlanta through the week driving back and forth.
It wasn't a really hard sell for the bank to be like, would you like to come back?
You've been in an apartment in Atlanta through the week for nine months now, 10 months now.
I'm like, yeah, I'd love to come back.
And then eventually jumped into Amazon.
At Amazon, I focused on, I was the lead of the Alexa data science team with an Alexa marketing science, very small team.

was two of us, myself and a data engineer, worked on building out personalization and trying to basically increase rates of sales of additional devices, cross-sell, and then understanding the different kinds of characteristics we had.

From there, moved into startup, started working extensively with LLMs, starting with sentiment analysis, and then moving all the way through kind of a rag search, more advanced rag search, where it made kind of a perplexity-style competitor.
Even had kind of a patent application there for an event monitor that I can say some about, but not too detailed about.
Their NDA was honestly terrifying.
And it was really refreshing to jump in.
was my first real deep dive into LLMs.
And I had done natural language processing a decade prior, and it was just awful, right?
I mean, you know, when they were talking sentiment analysis, I was doing background research on it, like, oh, wow, the last time I did this, you find a dictionary and everything.
Every word that's not stemmed or trunked or a transition word gets a positive or negative score.
Like this stuff's not great.
And then it had evolved into this semantic recognition.
And you could do tuning or training of smaller models or even use larger models for zero shot or multi-shot identification of what do we think the score is.
What did this startup do?
You mentioned on the Arisma, it's a stealth startup, but do you have any more?
I can tell you what I am allowed to say, because like I said, their disclosure agreement was stark, is we worked on an application called Nomi.
I believe it's still up.
Initially, we were crawling significant amounts of social media data, and then we moved on to news.
Probably the biggest contribution I made there was when I started exploring RAG search.
I developed an engine that looked very much like perplexity before we even knew, like perplexity wasn't a household name.
It had the Bezos money, but it
Hadn't gotten any of the BlackRock money.
We built this component where I'm like, RAG search.
Well, have all this news.

People could come in and ask questions about current events that an LLM has no knowledge of and generate informed responses that have itemized references to what we've collected in a given three-hour period.
It would constantly be fresh.
And during the competitor analysis identified, hey, there's this thing called perplexity that looks almost identical.
And I remember looking at the back-end API.
was like, well, this call looks almost like what I'm doing right now.
I think these are the same model architectures.
They evolved from there.
So I did the initial component there.
was a very hard ask.
was this sentiment analysis, but they were crawling just unreasonable amounts of social media data.
We're talking like multiple accounts from X where they were maxing out like multiple commercial accounts on a daily basis.
Everything daily from Reddit, multiple commercial accounts that were maxed.
And the owner came to me and said, Hey, I want to know the most important thing people are talking about every single three hours in the world.
And that's a very vague ask, right?
So that was part of the intrigue and part of the terror.
So I come in, I'm like, and she's like, and what do they think about it?
And she just said, I want you to do sentiment analysis.
That's it.
That's the only guidance.
So you're kind of that first product there was like, okay, wow.
First, have to define operational limits to start because we were crawling across like 20 languages.
And this was before there was expensive multi-language support, right?
So we were dealing with some of the smaller models that you're going to get off repos that have been open source up through.
I think they just released like a llama that was a 90B and that was hosted at some third parties.
OpenAI was out there, but we weren't really using it yet to start.
We ended up migrating it to, and I think it was in a two series or something.
Maybe three.
might be the first competent, but still, you get a lot of slop out of that.
And so what I ended up doing for that was taking a two-fold approach.
First, I'm going to define, we're going to start with English only, which is also 80% of the traffic we get in here.
Because you're looking at an American market, the American market is disproportionately English, that we don't have to worry about translation.
The second thing is we're going to then truncate what we look at as a meaningful post.
It has to have, and it's such an index or tier of followers.
We want to see reposts of it, but we don't want to follow reposts.
want the original materials.
So then you shrink it down.
Like the whole thing is you're trying to burn that haystack to get down to the actual critical material.
Once you're there, you still got an unreasonable amount of material.
So we said, okay, the next thing we need to do is twofold.
We need to figure it, break it into headlines and topics that are meaningful, and then figure out what it is people are saying about those headlines and topics.
And with that, it ended up.

Being a topic modeling approach, you go through and you do the very traditional like, okay, we've got this information coming in, we are going to embed it, then we're going to do some kind of variable reduction or manifold transformation, then we're going to cluster it, then once we have this into clusters, we're going to take headings and extract what is a headline for this, or what is a label that's meaningful, because if you're logging on, you don't want to see anything scientific or commas or keyword extractions.
You want to see people react to Minnesota ice unrest.
You're like, oh, wow, okay, that, you know, or violence erupts in North Pacific, something like that.
And then we were able to generate those.

Once we had them put it within those containers, then I tuned, I think at the time I ended up going with XML Roberta, because it did have multilingual support, and I wasn't sure how much they'd want to expand it.
I was able to find a labeled set of around 150,000 tweets.
And they're still called Twitter, but it was X.
had been open source for like Kaggle or something because I had no other labeled data, right?
We didn't have the person power to be able to go through and label 200,000 of them.
And I used that smaller model because at the time they didn't even have a dev server set up yet.
So I was on my Mac M2 using like some kind of, I think it was a package called Metal to leverage CUDA through the Apple Silicon.
And the thing I thought was going to melt through my desktop and I'm like, okay, retrain this half a billion parameter model tuned in locally.
And honestly, it did surprisingly well.
Like it wasn't as big of a load on it as I thought.
And then we were able to use that to say, okay, we now have positive, negative, neutral sentiment within each of these topics.
And I can give you here are the leading topics by what the volume of you is.
I think we also did a, if I remember right, a zero shot classification into kind
A classic reporting journalist, so it's like, this is sports, or this is international, or this is, you know, conflict kind of thing.
Yeah.
So the output of the model was a sentiment of, like, neutral, positive, and negative?
Yeah, yeah, it was binning.
In that particular case, so the aggregate output, had these cascading components, right?

So everything that went into it went through the initial input pipeline, and then the filtering system, and then it fell into one of the, you know, eight or ten buckets of, we're going to put this within, we didn't do these zero shot for individual tweets, we initially did the topic modeling, or sorry, yeah, topic modeling for individual tweets, so then they fall into a bucket, that headline gets put, is that sports, or is that international, or fashion, or something like that, and then the individual tweet was applied to sentiment analysis, right?
So you had, it was positive, negative, or neutral, so then when you looked and you saw unrest in Minneapolis...
It's based on ice.
It would float to the top if it was the most, you know, the biggest volume of tweets proportionally within U.S.
domestic politics or something like that.
And then of people talking about it, you know, 78% are unhappy, 8% are neutral, and the rest have positive sentiment around it.
And then it would have a headline attached so the user could go in and basically drill all the way down to here's the individual, most popular things people are talking about.
Here's the headlines.
Here's the overview of the headlines in terms of positivity, negativity, neutrality.
So, yeah, it was bucketed.
So what was the reason not to use, like, one of the packages?
Like, there's a couple of packages that were already available back then, right?
Like, at the NLTK or text blob, because they have this kind of, like, positive, negative, neutral, right?
And score?
Um, when we look.
I at it and evaluated it.
My particular concern was with those that I was aware of at the time.
I'd have to look at a list you gave me.
I wanted maximum flexibility.
I wanted to pull some kind of semantic meaning.
The ones that I reviewed at the time were more mechanical.
They weren't just strictly like you might have not just skinned a single word.
It gets a positive five.
The next one gets a negative two.
There were maybe engrams or bigrams or something in there.
But knowing that it was on social media, you're not going to have this classic structure that you'd have in journalism or textbooks.
There's going to be a lot of slang.
There's going to be a lot of contextual information.

I wanted to rely as much on the semantic structure that would be within the LLM or any kind of embeddings versus any kind of a dictionary, engram lookup, what that sequence of words generally would get as a positive, negative, or neutral.
And that's why I went for it.
And at the time, I didn't see that there were any, there were a couple of pre-trained sentiment.
But when I tested them against, those were just on hugging face, honestly, the users that uploaded.
When I tested them against what we were able to get to establish a benchmark, which like the F1 score wasn't crazy.
The accuracy was, I mean, it was like 0.8 accuracy or something like that.
I mean, it was, you know, it was good, but not great, but it was acceptable.
It was fieldable.
And when then you did the initial inspections, it looked good.
But that was the reason we went with that.
I wanted an LLM approach.
And the other reason was because, to be honest, owner very much wanted AI, LLM-driven content.
So when I hear that, I'm like, we're going to train an LLM for this because that's what you asked for.
Makes sense.
And what happened with the company?
So they never got out of the stealth mode?
They're still there, to my knowledge.
I don't have any official facts on it.
But they, the reason I ended up jumping is, I mean, they compensated well.
The reason I jumped...
Out of it was because twofold.
One, work-life balance was non-existent.
I mean, we're talking, we didn't use calendars.
We didn't use email.
Your phone was on 24 hours a day.
I'd have my daughter at Disneyland Friday at six o'clock.
The owner call like, hey, why aren't you on this call that I just scheduled five minutes ago?
Stuff like that.
Like I'd get calls Saturday morning at one in the morning, stuff like that.
And the other was they were incredibly well-funded, but they kept pivoting.
Like I worked on eight or nine projects in say the 12 or 15 months or whatever I was there.
And they kept changing.
So I think they, I heard rumors.
don't know for sure that they'd burned in the three years, $30 million.
And we didn't have a single paying client.
So even when I left, like I'd been offered 0.8 equity, like almost a full percent equity ownership.
And I was just like, I don't see this as being viable.
Plus the turnover the first six months, I pretended I was in a reality.
So there were about 20 of us and they fired 5% of the company a week.
Like you'd come on and there'd just be like a rage firing a week.
And I just sat there like.
Okay, you know, like I've been through nastier situations, but this isn't long-term viable.
That's why, honestly, we made really interesting stuff and they had good staff.
The data engineer was a particle physicist, PhD, that had done his postdoc at CERN.
I mean, they had like really good people there with a lot of experience.
just, the way it was run didn't really materialize, it felt like.
Yeah, and bummer that like after all of this work, there's still, they don't have clients.
They don't have launch product.
So after you moved to your last current role, tell me about this company and about your tasks and projects.
Sure, at MDLynx right now, I've got several.
One is heavy on AI automation, right?
It's how can you build agents and I've got, it's not very restrictive.
I can talk like automation.
We get a ton of requests for proposals and the company essentially generates what I call like kind of synopsis or readers digest material for physicians.
The idea is we get together a group of physicians, tens of thousands, more actually, that read us on a regular basis.
And it's everything from like celebrity interest to what's Robert Redford sick with to here's this new GLP-1 treatment to here's a malpractice thing that you might want to worry about.
Here's how you manage your practice.
You've got, you know, 15 or 20 different topics they work within.
You get a readership base.
And then the way we generate revenue is the pharmaceuticals companies come in and say, well, I've got a new heart drug.
How many cardiologists do you have?
Can you help me distribute information to educate them regarding the effects of this new heart drug we would like to target?
And we've got a lot of information regarding who they are.
People that are in a high concentration of greater than average.
Cardiovascular risk treatment in a geographical region among cardiologists, among lower socioeconomic status, whatever it is, higher socioeconomic status.
And then basically they sell advertising through this platform, not Dissembler from a newspaper or any other website.
They just do it mainly through email, which is a limitation.
So one of the main projects that I've been working on was this request for proposal automation.
They get a lot of requests for proposals, and it takes a lot of hours to respond to those with a high level of detail.
And we've got a pretty small team, even though it's part of a big corporation.
It is kind of a small team and a small company within a big corporation.
So we needed to find efficiencies so they could respond to more.
Better details.
So I built, and I can go into as much detail as you want, basically a multi-agent approach that took in previous examples, generated a roadmap, took directions,
We a template and said, okay, well, here's a menu of all the things we do.
And here's the details about this.
Go research this disease state.
Go research what it is we've done previously.
Here's an example of how we responded to an RFP.
Make a robot and then iterates through making a series of slides, populates it in a slide deck, hands it off.
So, you know, I call it a washing machine, not a robot.
It doesn't take 100% of the work out and it wasn't intended to.
It's intended to come in and 90% of what you had to do is taken into this machine.
So then be a business specialist to put them together and do something else.

Another line of work I've been in was we basically synthesized the concept that came up with what if we put a team of editorial staff at every major medical conference we want them to cover because there's many, many booths and abstracts, thousands of abstracts.
There's no way to get through all of it.
And what if we covered it, figured out what the...
Dominant features and topics of discussion were summarized.
essentially generated a response page as if we had almost a limitless number of reporters on the ground covering it.
And then that could be used to drive traffic to the site and to float to the top, you know, key opinion leaders, things like that, different featured content.
And we've actually got that moving forward pretty well.
And then the third was a content recommendation engine, which has become both interesting and problematic because we don't have a terribly high click-through rate.
And it's within a very specific subgroup.
We're an email format, so I probably shouldn't say because it's kind of internal information whether it's are they younger or older, but different channels have different affinities.
People younger like social media, people that are older like something else.
So you've kind of, I'm helping walk them out of this kind of way.
What?
But I've started to think of like a local minima where they're reinforced in this really small group and they're giving what the group wants at the expense of serving everyone else.

And that comes from everything from content personalization through building out an embedding engine and what can we find that's similar through segmentation and extracting out what's most popular among certain segments, whether that be professional specialty or age or geographical region and things of that nature.
Those are the three main projects.
I mean, I've had honestly, in the time I've been there, a large group of other projects to work on.
But yeah, that's been the three main projects and it's, it's, we've had some pretty solid successes at points with the marketing and some of the tests.
How big is the team you work on?
It's very small.
I work directly with their, I forget exactly what it's titled, chief technology officer.
And then they've got their principal.
I've to engineer.
And it's basically the three of us.
And then I've got an engineering lead that I partner with as we move some things back and forth, because it'll come from, I'll have like, I cover the full stack of it, right?
Where it'll come from conceptualization through me getting a good idea and saying, hey, maybe we try this.
Leadership says, go try it.
So I rapidly prototype.
As I've rapidly prototyped, I have to go find the data and pull it in.
And then I start having an eye towards how do I build this to ensure it's scalable, repeatable, and clean, and goes between all these different environments we have to work in.
And so then I'm simultaneously pushing it out to clients for the initial look, as we start talking about, do we move this into a production build in the stack?
And then so I'll usually take it so far, depending on how he wants to encapsulate it, he being the chief tech officer, that's also kind of their lead engineer on all things production.
And then we collaborate to take it to the last 10 yards.
then he'll kick it back and we usually end up changing things.
But yeah, that's the team.
It's not a massive team.
The overall company might be 15 or 20 people.
Okay.
And then you said that it's part of the larger corporation?
Yeah, it's a corporation called M3.
M3 is actually a publicly traded Japanese company, but we're in M3 USA.

And then M3 USA has, I don't know how many, maybe a dozen, two dozen companies, many of which they've acquired that were smaller companies, us being one of them, that I think it had been 20-some people were acquired maybe a decade ago and haven't really grown in staff, but they're trying to scale up with AI efficiencies currently.
Got it, got it.
Makes sense.
And maybe you can share also with me, like you shared a bunch of projects, but maybe one that you heard a problem and you found a solution.
Yeah, a how-to.
How
Tackle this problem.
And you also saw business impact at the end of application.
So if there's something like that you have in your recent project portfolio, we'd love to hear about that.
Okay, sure.
I think that I've covered most of the work that I did at the startup.
And I've given you a high-level overview of what I'm working on in the contract.
One of my favorites, and this will be going back maybe five years, but it was at Synovus.
And it was also part of the reason I like it so much, it was before a lot of the modern tools were out.
